revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine}}[[Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] is a shrine located in Nagasaki, Nagasaki Prefecture, Japan. It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died from the Meiji Restoration to the Pacific War (World War II).
The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Umegasaki Shokon Shrine, dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the Boshin War. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the Taiwan Expedition. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Ken Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the current shrine buildings being completed in 1944. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26.
[[File:Nagasaki_Shokon_sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokon Shrine (Nagasaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine) Nagasaki hand-colored postcard]]
== History == In 1869, the Umegasaki Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Umegasaki, to enshrine 43 vassals who died in the Bunkyū War. In 1874, the Sako Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Nishi-shima, to enshrine 536 soldiers who died in the Taiwan Expedition, and since then it has enshrined the war dead. In 1939, under the system reform of the Shokon Shrine, both the Umegasaki and Sako Shokon Shrines became Gokoku Shrines. In March 1942, the two Gokoku Shrines were merged and became the Nagasaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, designated by the Minister of the Interior. The shrine hall was built at its current location and completed and relocated in March 1944.
In August 1945, all buildings were lost due to the atomic bombing of Nagasaki. The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokon Shrine. It was rebuilt in October 1963. {{Shinto}}{{shinto-stub}} [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine}}[[Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26.
[[File:Nagasaki_Shokon_sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokon Shrine (Nagasaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine) Nagasaki hand-colored postcard]]
== History == In 1869, the Umegasaki Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Umegasaki, to enshrine 43 vassals who died in the Bunkyū War. In 1874, the Sako Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Nishi-shima, to enshrine 536 soldiers who died in the Taiwan Expedition, and since then it has enshrined the war dead. In 1939, under the system reform of the Shokon Shrine, both the Umegasaki and Sako Shokon Shrines became Gokoku Shrines. In March 1942, the two Gokoku Shrines were merged and became the Nagasaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, designated by the Minister of the Interior. The shrine hall was built at its current location and completed and relocated in March 1944.
In August 1945, all buildings were lost due to the atomic bombing of Nagasaki. The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokon Shrine. It was rebuilt in October 1963. {{Shinto}}{{shinto-stub}} [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine}}[[Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.<ref>{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}</ref> It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died from the [[Meiji Restoration]] to the [[Pacific War]] (World War II).
The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Umegasaki Shokon Shrine, dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Ken Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the current shrine buildings being completed in 1944. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26.
[[File:Nagasaki_Shokon_sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokon Shrine (Nagasaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine) Nagasaki hand-colored postcard]]
== History == In 1869, the Umegasaki Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Umegasaki, to enshrine 43 vassals who died in the Bunkyū War. In 1874, the Sako Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Nishi-shima, to enshrine 536 soldiers who died in the Taiwan Expedition, and since then it has enshrined the war dead. In 1939, under the system reform of the Shokon Shrine, both the Umegasaki and Sako Shokon Shrines became Gokoku Shrines. In March 1942, the two Gokoku Shrines were merged and became the Nagasaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, designated by the Minister of the Interior. The shrine hall was built at its current location and completed and relocated in March 1944.
In August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]]. The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokon Shrine. It was rebuilt in October 1963.

It was rebuilt in October 1963. == See Also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] }} {{shinto-stub}} {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=January 2023}}

{{AfC submission|t||ts=20230122122211|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=January 2023}} {{notability|date=January 2023}} }}{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 長崎護国神社 - panoramio.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Ill|Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|長崎縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died from the [[Meiji Restoration]] to the [[Pacific War]] (World War II). The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Umegasaki Shokon Shrine, dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Ken Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the current shrine buildings being completed in 1944. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This pghoto depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] In 1869, the Umegasaki Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Umegasaki, to enshrine 43 vassals who died in the Bunkyū War. In 1874, the Sako Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Nishi-shima, to enshrine 536 soldiers who died in the Taiwan Expedition, and since then it has enshrined the war dead. In 1939, under the system reform of the Shokon Shrine, both the Umegasaki and Sako Shokon Shrines became Gokoku Shrines. In March 1942, the two Gokoku Shrines were merged and became the Nagasaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, designated by the Minister of the Interior. The shrine hall was built at its current location and completed and relocated in March 1944. In August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]]. The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokon Shrine. It was rebuilt in October 1963. == See Also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] }} {{shinto-stub}} {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=January 2023}}

{{AFC submission|d|v|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|decliner=Asilvering|declinets=20230606003406|ts=20230422203207}} {{AFC comment|1=History section does not have sources specified [[User:Asilvering|asilvering]] ([[User talk:Asilvering|talk]]) 00:34, 6 June 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shrine in Nagasaki}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=January 2023}} {{notability|date=January 2023}} }}{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 長崎護国神社 - panoramio.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1869 | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Ill|Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|長崎縣護國神社|simple}} is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died from the [[Meiji Restoration]] to the [[Pacific War]] (World War II). The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the {{Ill|Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|長崎縣護國神社}} in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]]. The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokon Shrine. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This pghoto depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] In 1869, the Umegasaki Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Umegasaki, to enshrine 43 vassals who died in the Bunkyū War. In 1874, the Sako Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Nishi-shima, to enshrine 536 soldiers who died in the Taiwan Expedition, and since then it has enshrined the war dead. In 1939, under the system reform of the Shokon Shrine, both the Umegasaki and Sako Shokon Shrines became Gokoku Shrines. In March 1942, the two Gokoku Shrines were merged and became the Nagasaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, designated by the Minister of the Interior. The shrine hall was built at its current location and completed and relocated in March 1944. In August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]]. The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokon Shrine. It was rebuilt in October 1963. == See Also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Draft categories| [[:Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[:Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[:Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[:Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[:Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[:Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[:Category:1869 establishments]] }} {{shinto-stub}} {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=January 2023}}

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Ill|Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|長崎縣護國神社|simple}} is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died from the [[Meiji Restoration]] to the [[Pacific War]] (World War II). The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the {{Ill|Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|長崎縣護國神社}} in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]]. The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokon Shrine. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This pghoto depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] In 1869, the Umegasaki Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Umegasaki, to enshrine 43 vassals who died in the Bunkyū War. In 1874, the Sako Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Nishi-shima, to enshrine 536 soldiers who died in the Taiwan Expedition, and since then it has enshrined the war dead. In 1939, under the system reform of the Shokon Shrine, both the Umegasaki and Sako Shokon Shrines became Gokoku Shrines. In March 1942, the two Gokoku Shrines were merged and became the Nagasaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, designated by the Minister of the Interior. The shrine hall was built at its current location and completed and relocated in March 1944. In August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]]. The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokon Shrine. It was rebuilt in October 1963. == See Also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Draft categories| [[:Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[:Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[:Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[:Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[:Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[:Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[:Category:1869 establishments]] }} {{shinto-stub}} {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=January 2023}}

Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]]. The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokon Shrine. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This pghoto depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] In 1869, the Umegasaki Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Umegasaki, to enshrine 43 vassals who died in the Bunkyū War. In 1874, the Sako Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Nishi-shima, to enshrine 536 soldiers who died in the Taiwan Expedition, and since then it has enshrined the war dead. In 1939, under the system reform of the Shokon Shrine, both the Umegasaki and Sako Shokon Shrines became Gokoku Shrines. In March 1942, the two Gokoku Shrines were merged and became the Nagasaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, designated by the Minister of the Interior. The shrine hall was built at its current location and completed and relocated in March 1944. In August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]]. The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokon Shrine. It was rebuilt in October 1963. == See Also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines }}{{shinto-stub}}

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:長崎縣護國神社]][[simple:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Ill|Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|長崎縣護國神社|simple}} is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=Frank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PT134&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&hl=en |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA100&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&hl=en |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died from the [[Meiji Restoration]] to the [[Pacific War]] (World War II). The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the {{Ill|Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|長崎縣護國神社}} in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]]. The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokon Shrine. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] In 1869, the Umegasaki Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Umegasaki, to enshrine 43 vassals who died in the Bunkyū War. In 1874, the Sako Shokon Shrine was built in Nagasaki City, Nishi-shima, to enshrine 536 soldiers who died in the Taiwan Expedition, and since then it has enshrined the war dead. In 1939, under the system reform of the Shokon Shrine, both the Umegasaki and Sako Shokon Shrines became Gokoku Shrines. In March 1942, the two Gokoku Shrines were merged and became the Nagasaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine, designated by the Minister of the Interior. The shrine hall was built at its current location and completed and relocated in March 1944. In August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokon Shrine. It was rebuilt in October 1963. == See Also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Draft articles]] }} {{shinto-stub}}

The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See Also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Draft articles]] }} {{shinto-stub}}

{{Short description|Shrine in Nagasaki}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{AfC submission|||ts=20231128073301|u=Immanuelle|ns=118}} {{AFC submission|d|v|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|decliner=Asilvering|declinets=20230606003406|ts=20230422203207}} {{AFC comment|1=History section does not have sources specified [[User:Asilvering|asilvering]] ([[User talk:Asilvering|talk]]) 00:34, 6 June 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=January 2023}} {{notability|date=January 2023}} }}{{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 長崎護国神社 - panoramio.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan[[ja:長崎縣護國神社]][[simple:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = 1869 | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:長崎縣護國神社]][[simple:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Ill|Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|長崎縣護國神社|simple}} is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=Frank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PT134&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&hl=en |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA100&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&hl=en |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA100&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&hl=en |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died from the [[Meiji Restoration]] to the [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See Also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Draft articles]] }} {{shinto-stub}}

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=Frank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PT134&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&hl=en |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA100&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&hl=en |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&newbks=0&printsec=frontcover&pg=PA100&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&hl=en |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died from the [[Meiji Restoration]] to the [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Draft articles]] {{Shinto-stub}}

Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] {{Shinto-stub}}

Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died from the [[Meiji Restoration]] to the [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Dd]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia4]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Short description|Shrine in Nagasaki}} {{Copy edit|for=reduing the See also section|date=November 2023}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 長崎護国神社 - panoramio.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|41.61|N|129|51|19.59|E}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = 41-67 Joeimachi, [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]] | festival = | consecration_year = 1869 | type = [[Gokoku shrine]]
(formerly [[Shokonsha]]) | leadership = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | website = {{Official|https://www.yasukuni.or.jp/gokoku.html}} }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|長崎護国神社}}) is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=Frank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PT134 |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia10]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:ファイル]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. (formerly [[Shokonsha]]) | leadership = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | website = {{Official|https://www.yasukuni.or.jp/gokoku.html}} }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|長崎護国神社}}) is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=Frank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PT134 |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq\]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Short description|Shrine in Nagasaki}} {{Copy edit|for=reduing the See also section|date=November 2023}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 長崎護国神社 - panoramio.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|41.61|N|129|51|19.59|E}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = 41-67 Joeimachi, [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]] | festival = | consecration_year = 1869 | type = [[Gokoku shrine]]
(formerly [[Shokonsha]]) | leadership = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | website = {{Official|https://www.yasukuni.or.jp/gokoku.html}} }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|長崎護国神社}}) is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=Frank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PT134 |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq\]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}}

It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[category:History of Nagasaki]] [[category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[category:1869 establishments]] [[category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:qq]] [[category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:qq]] [[category:bump-01-18]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]] [[category:qq--qq]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]] [[category:qq--qq]] [[category:kk]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[category:qq\]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Shrine in Nagasaki}} {{Copy edit|for=reduing the See also section|date=November 2023}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 長崎護国神社 - panoramio.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|41.61|N|129|51|19.59|E}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = 41-67 Joeimachi, [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]] | festival = | consecration_year = 1869 | type = [[Gokoku shrine]]
(formerly [[Shokonsha]]) | leadership = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | website = {{Official|https://www.yasukuni.or.jp/gokoku.html}} }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|長崎護国神社}}) is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=Frank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PT134 |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[category:History of Nagasaki]] [[category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[category:1869 establishments]] [[category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:qq]] [[category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:qq]] [[category:bump-01-18]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]][[Category:qq 8]] [[category:qq--qq]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]][[Category:qq 8]] [[category:qq--qq]] [[category:kk]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[category:qq\]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]][[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]]\[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
