(formerly [[Shokonsha]]) | leadership = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | website = {{Official|https://www.yasukuni.or.jp/gokoku.html}} }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|長崎護国神社}}) is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=Frank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PT134 |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[category:History of Nagasaki]] [[category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[category:1869 establishments]] [[category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:qq]] [[category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:qq]] [[category:bump-01-18]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]][[Category:qq 8]] [[category:qq--qq]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]][[Category:qq 8]] [[category:qq--qq]] [[category:kk]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[category:qq\]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]][[Category:New]] \[[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:qq 2]]\[[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:New]]

The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[category:History of Nagasaki]] [[category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[category:1869 establishments]] [[category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:qq]] [[category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:qq]] [[category:bump-01-18]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]][[Category:qq 8]] [[category:qq--qq]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]][[Category:qq 8]] [[category:qq--qq]] [[category:kk]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[category:qq\]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]][[Category:New]] \[[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 2]]\[[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]]

{{Short description|Shrine in Nagasaki}} {{Copy edit|for=reduing the See also section|date=November 2023}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 長崎護国神社 - panoramio.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|41.61|N|129|51|19.59|E}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = 41-67 Joeimachi, [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]] | festival = | consecration_year = 1869 | type = [[Gokoku shrine]]
(formerly [[Shokonsha]]) | leadership = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | website = {{Official|https://www.yasukuni.or.jp/gokoku.html}} }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|長崎護国神社}}) is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=Frank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PT134 |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[category:History of Nagasaki]] [[category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[category:1869 establishments]] [[category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:qq]] [[category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:qq]] [[category:bump-01-18]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]][[Category:qq 8]] [[category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]][[Category:qq 8]] [[category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[category:kk]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[category:qq\]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]][[Category:New]] \[[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 2]]\[[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]]

(formerly [[Shokonsha]]) | leadership = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | website = {{Official|https://www.yasukuni.or.jp/gokoku.html}} }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|長崎護国神社}}) is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=Frank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PT134 |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} [[category:History of Nagasaki]] [[category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[category:Gokoku shrines]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[category:1869 establishments]] [[category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:qq]] [[category:qq]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:qq]] [[category:bump-01-18]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]][[Category:qq 8]] [[category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]][[Category:qq 8]] [[category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[category:kk]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[category:qq\]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]][[Category:New]] \[[Category:Cer 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 2]]\[[Category:Cer 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. {{Short description|Shrine in Nagasaki}} {{Copy edit|for=reduing the See also section|date=November 2023}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 長崎護国神社 - panoramio.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|41.61|N|129|51|19.59|E}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = 41-67 Joeimachi, [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]] | festival = | consecration_year = 1869 | type = [[Gokoku shrine]]
(formerly [[Shokonsha]]) | leadership = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | website = {{Official|https://www.yasukuni.or.jp/gokoku.html}} }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|長崎護国神社}}) is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=Frank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PT134 |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Short description|Shrine in Nagasaki}} {{Copy edit|for=reduing the See also section|date=November 2023}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 長崎護国神社 - panoramio.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|41.61|N|129|51|19.59|E}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = 41-67 Joeimachi, [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]] | festival = | consecration_year = 1869 | type = [[Gokoku shrine]]
(formerly [[Shokonsha]]) | leadership = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | website = {{Official|https://www.yasukuni.or.jp/gokoku.html}} }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|長崎護国神社}}) is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=Frank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PT134 |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11\]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Cer 5]] [[Category: 5]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[ja:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]]

Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Cer 5]] [[Category: 5]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[ja:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]]

Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] }} [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[ja:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]]

(formerly [[Shokonsha]]) | leadership = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | website = {{Official|https://www.yasukuni.or.jp/gokoku.html}} }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|長崎護国神社}}) is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=FRank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PT134 |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[ja:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]]

Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[sn:agasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Ja import failed]]

{{Short description|Shrine in Nagasaki}} {{Copy edit|for=reduing the See also section|date=November 2023}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 長崎護国神社 - panoramio.jpg | caption = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|32|46|41.61|N|129|51|19.59|E}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = 41-67 Joeimachi, [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]] | festival = | consecration_year = 1869 | type = [[Gokoku shrine]]
(formerly [[Shokonsha]]) | leadership = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | website = {{Official|https://www.yasukuni.or.jp/gokoku.html}} }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|長崎護国神社}}) is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=FRank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PT134 |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[sn:agasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[sn: failed]]

(formerly [[Shokonsha]]) | leadership = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | website = {{Official|https://www.yasukuni.or.jp/gokoku.html}} }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|長崎護国神社}}) is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=FRank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PT134 |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[sn:agasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Ja import failed]]

Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] }} [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[sn:agasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn: failed]]

Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] }} [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[sn:agasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn: failed]]

Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[sn:agasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Ja import failed]]

Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[sn:agasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Ja import failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Nagasakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[ja:長崎縣護國神社]] [[simple:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]]

Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] [[Category:Completed pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[sn:agasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Ja import failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Nagasakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[ja:長崎縣護國神社]] [[simple:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

(formerly [[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]) | leadership = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | website = {{Official|https://www.yasukuni.or.jp/gokoku.html}} }} '''Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine''' ({{lang-ja|長崎護国神社}}) is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in [[Nagasaki]], [[Nagasaki Prefecture]], Japan.{{Cite web |title=長崎で「護国神社の庭フェス」 徒歩来場者には特典も |url=https://nagasaki.keizai.biz/headline/1405/ |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=長崎経済新聞}}{{Cite web |title=長崎県護国神社 {{!}} {{!}} ながさきの平和【公式】 |url=https://nagasakipeace.jp/gokoku.html |access-date=2023-01-09 |website=ながさきの平和 |language=ja}}{{Cite book |last=Chinnock |first=FRank W. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z01FEAAAQBAJ&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PT134 |title=Nagasaki: The Forgotten Bomb |date=2021-11-21 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-000-45899-2 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=Nagasaki+Gokoku&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Sturgeon |first=William Daniel |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zHC6f08Qi5sC&dq=nagasaki+gokoku+shrine&pg=PA100 |title=Japan's Yasukuni Shrine: Place of Peace Or Place of Conflict? Regional Politics of History and Memory in East Asia |date=2006 |publisher=Universal-Publishers |isbn=978-1-58112-334-0 |language=en}} It is dedicated to the spirits of the approximately 60,000 people from Nagasaki Prefecture who died between the [[Meiji Restoration]] and [[Pacific War]] (World War II). It is dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the casualties of wars, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[:en:Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] }} [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[sn:agasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Nagasakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[ja:長崎縣護國神社]] [[simple:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |doi=10.1093/jcs/32.3.527 |jstor=23917081 |issn=0021-969X}} == History == [[File:Nagasaki Shokon sha.jpg|thumb|Nagasaki Shokonsha. This photo depicts the old shrine, and it was completely destroyed in the atomic bombing]] The shrine was founded in 1869 in the Umegasaki district of Nagasaki as the Nagasaki [[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]], dedicated to the 43 samurais who died in the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. In 1874, the Saku Shokon Shrine was established in the Nishi-shima district of Nagasaki to honor the 536 people who died in the [[:en:Japanese invasion of Taiwan (1874)|Taiwan Expedition]]. Both shrines merged and became the Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine in 1942, with the shrine buildings being completed in 1944. Then in August 1945, all of the shrine's buildings were destroyed in the [[:en:Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]].{{Cite web |title=Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine - Hiroshima - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/91240/Hiroshima-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-11-10 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}}{{Rp|page=121}} The Sako Shokon Shrine (Umegasaki Tenno-miya Shrine) was temporarily located at the site of the former Sako Shokonsha. It was rebuilt in October 1963. It is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] }} [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[sn:agasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Nagasakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[ja:長崎縣護國神社]] [[simple:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

The shrine's annual festivals are held on April 22 and October 26. == See also == * [[Hiroshima Gokoku Shrine]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == References == {{Reflist}}{{Shinto}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| [[Category:History of Nagasaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nagasaki]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:1869 establishments]] }} [[sn:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:長崎護国神社]] [[sn:agasaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Nagasakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[ja:長崎縣護國神社]] [[simple:Nagasaki Gokoku Shrine]]
